label: verify-220-cal-app-4th-586
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- 220 Cal.App.4th 586

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …uthority.** One panelist relied on *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130 as tolerating conservation-oriented tiered pricing; four others cited *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586. These are different cases with different holdings being used for a similar purpose.

4. **What *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* stands for.** One panelist cited it for the agency's burden of proof by a preponderance; anothe…

[tetlock] …ionale removes tiered pricing from Art. XIII D scrutiny — undercuts the district's Water Code §375/Art. X §2 exemption theory as a classification-level escape hatch.
- Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 is the district's best authority for a 'not property-related' argument (groundwater extraction charge held outside Art. XIII D because tied to an act of extraction, not ownership), but it is a Court of Appeal decision a…

[dialectic] …perfectly cost-justified in the aggregate, because the *use* of the revenue, not just its *amount*, independently breaches 6(b)(1)/(b)(2)/(b)(3).
- Analogize to *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586, which examined whether augmentation-fee revenues were spent in a manner that correlated with the burden fee-payors placed on the resource, rather than cross-subsidizing unrelated programs (verify precise holding/applic…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"MISCHARACTERIZED","canonical_cite":"Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586","note":"The case exists and the cite (reporter/year/court) is correct; Griffith actually held the augmentation charge IS a property-related fee under Art. XIII D (imposed as an incident of ownership), exempt only from Prop 218's voter-approval requirement via the water-service carve-out, and separately upheld the agency's revenue/cost-allocation methodology (supporting the [dialectic] use). The [tetlock] snippet inverts this, calling Griffith authority for a 'not property-related/extraction not ownership' argument — that rationale actually belongs to City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191, which expressly disapproved Griffith on this point.","source_urls":["https://caselaw.findlaw.com/ca-court-of-appeal/1646971.html","https://blog.aklandlaw.com/2013/10/articles/exactions-impact-fees-service-charges-and-property-taxes/court-affirms-groundwater-augmentation-charge-exempt-from-proposition-218-as-a-water-service-charge/","https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/city-san-buenaventura-v-united-water-conservation-district-34548","https://www.bwslaw.com/publications/water-service-fee-increases-under-proposition-218-proper-procedure-after-griffith-v-pajaro-valley-water-management/"]}

data.canonical_cite: Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Management Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586
data.note: The case exists and the cite (reporter/year/court) is correct; Griffith actually held the augmentation charge IS a property-related fee under Art. XIII D (imposed as an incident of ownership), exempt only from Prop 218's voter-approval requirement via the water-service carve-out, and separately upheld the agency's revenue/cost-allocation methodology (supporting the [dialectic] use). The [tetlock] snippet inverts this, calling Griffith authority for a 'not property-related/extraction not ownership' argument — that rationale actually belongs to City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191, which expressly disapproved Griffith on this point.
